title: ECIClassificationResult
description: Structured output the ECI (Enterprise Context Intelligence) classifier must return for a given prompt. This describes context only - it is NOT a decision. The Policy Engine, not this schema, decides Allow/Warn/Mask/Block.

intent.description: What the user is trying to do with this prompt.

documentType.description: The kind of enterprise content this prompt most resembles, if any.

requiresEnterpriseKnowledge.description: True if answering this prompt correctly requires knowledge specific to the enterprise (its products, systems, or internal processes) rather than general public knowledge.

containsInternalArchitecture.description: True if the prompt describes or references internal system architecture, service names, infrastructure layout, or design decisions specific to the enterprise.

containsImplementationDetails.description: True if the prompt reveals how something is actually implemented internally (algorithms, business logic, configuration specifics) beyond a generic/public explanation.

containsSourceCode.description: True if the prompt includes or asks about actual proprietary source code, not generic/public code samples.

containsCustomerData.description: True if the prompt references real or plausible customer-specific data (accounts, transactions, identifiers) rather than generic/hypothetical examples.

containsSecrets.description: Defense-in-depth backstop only - true if the prompt appears to contain secrets/credentials that regex or Presidio may have missed. This is NOT the primary detection path for secrets; Regex and Presidio layers own that responsibility.

confidence.description: Model's self-reported confidence in this classification. Self-reported confidence from a small local model is not fully reliable on its own - treat as one signal among several, not a sole gate.

reasoning.description: Short, plain-language justifications for the classification above. Used by the Policy Engine to build a human-readable explanation, and for debugging/sanity-checking the model's output.
